frameworks.name: signal detection theory
frameworks.one_line: uses d' to index objective sensitivity separately from confidence reports

frameworks.name: subjective awareness vs objective performance dissociation
frameworks.one_line: frames confidence as a marker of subjective visibility distinct from sensitivity

frameworks.name: blindsight
frameworks.one_line: cited as canonical case of perception-performance dissociation

core_question: Can confidence judgments dissociate from objective discrimination performance for matched central versus peripheral stimuli in neurologically intact observers?
key_assumptions: d' indexes objective sensitivity independently of subjective report
confidence comparison across simultaneous stimuli probes subjective visibility
matched d' implies equated objective signal strength
manipulations: stimulus eccentricity (central vs peripheral dot motion)
motion coherence to titrate peripheral and central d' to equality
key_findings: strong bias toward reporting higher confidence in central stimulus
central confidence bias persists at the psychometric point of equal d'
objective discrimination performance can be matched while subjective confidence diverges
interpretations: paradigm dissociates subjective visual awareness from objective signal strength in normal observers
central-peripheral confidence asymmetry offers a psychophysical handle on subjective visibility
